back: A Hasse diagram is a graphical representation of a partially ordered set (poset). It is a type of directed acyclic graph where vertices represent elements of the poset, and edges represent the covering relation. All implied relationships from reflexivity and transitivity, as well as the direction arrows (by drawing the graph so that all edges point upwards), are omitted for simplicity.

front: What is the 'covering relation' central to drawing a Hasse diagram?
back: For elements a and b in a poset (P, ≤), we say that 'b covers a' if a < b and there is no element c in P such that a < c < b. In the Hasse diagram, an edge is drawn from a to b if and only if b covers a.

front: List the steps to draw a Hasse diagram for a given poset (P, ≤).
back:
1. Remove all self-loops (reflexivity).
2. Remove all edges implied by transitivity.
3. Draw the diagram so that all remaining edges point upward (removing direction arrows).
4. Place elements such that if a ≤ b, then b is placed higher than a in the diagram.

front: In a Hasse diagram, what do the concepts of 'maximal' and 'minimal' elements refer to?
back: A maximal element is one that has no other element greater than it (nothing above it in the Hasse diagram). A minimal element is one that has no other element less than it (nothing below it in the Hasse diagram). A poset can have multiple maximal and minimal elements.

front: Identify the maximal, minimal, greatest, and least elements in the Hasse diagram of the poset ({2,3,4,5,6}, |) (divisibility relation).
back:
Minimal elements: 2, 3, 5 (nothing divides them from below in the set).
Maximal elements: 4, 5, 6 (they are not divisors of any other number in the set besides themselves).
There is no greatest element (an element divisible by all others) because no single element is above both 4 and 5 and 6.
There is no least element (an element that divides all others) because no single element is below both 2 and 3 and 5.
